preFix.title: Using seaweed as a compost additive and soil conditioner
preFix.subtitle: When and how to add seaweed to the heap or apply it directly
preFix.excerpt: Freshly collected seaweed is a high-mineral compost activator with a carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of around 10:1, similar to fresh grass clippings. It contains trace elements (iodine, magnesium, sulphur) that most composts lack and natural growth-promoting alginates. In a heap it decomposes within weeks. Applied directly as a mulch, it breaks down slowly, suppresses weeds, and releases minerals through winter.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS guidance on using seaweed in the garden 2024. Scottish Natural Heritage guidance on seaweed collection from beaches (no permit required for personal amounts under 200 kg wet weight per year in Scotland; similar guidance applies in England and Wales). ADAS nutrient data for seaweed compost.

content.text: Freshly harvested seaweed, bladder wrack, kelp, sea lettuce, decomposes rapidly in a warm compost heap, contributing nitrogen, trace minerals, and

that standard kitchen-waste compost lacks. Its salt content, the main concern when using seaweed, is washed out after the first rainfall or a thorough rinse before use. Fresh seaweed from a beach has a salt concentration of around 0.5 to 1.0% by dry weight. After one heavy rain or overnight rinsing in a bucket, this drops to below 0.1%, within the acceptable range for most plants.

Collect from the strandline (freshly deposited material, not rock-attached living seaweed). In England and Wales, taking seaweed from the strandline for personal garden use does not require a permit. Do not remove attached seaweeds from rocks, these are living organisms and part of the intertidal habitat. Take no more than you can use; left in a bin bag at home for more than a day, seaweed heats rapidly, develops an ammonia smell, and begins to liquefy. Use within 24 hours or freeze in small bags.
Rinse fresh seaweed in a bucket of fresh water to reduce salt. Drain well.
Add to the heap in thin layers (50 to 100 mm), alternating with carbon materials (straw, card). Seaweed mats into a dense layer if added too thickly, blocking air.
Mixed into an active heap, seaweed will be unrecognisable within 2 to 4 weeks in summer, 6 to 8 weeks in autumn.

Rinsed seaweed applied directly to bare soil as a 50 to 75 mm mulch in autumn acts as a slow-release mineral source and light weed suppressor. It breaks down through winter, improving soil structure with the alginate content. By spring it is partially decomposed and can be worked in or left as a surface dressing. This is particularly effective around fruit bushes and in the kitchen garden when the beds have been cleared after harvest.
Dried seaweed meal (available from garden centres) is a convenient alternative to fresh. It has a

of around 30 to 40:1 (drier than fresh) and is added at around 35 g/m2 as a soil amendment. Liquid seaweed extract (kelp extract) is used as a foliar spray or root drench at the concentration specified by the product, typically 1:40. These products are not fungicides or pesticides; their benefit is micro-nutrient provision and growth-regulator activity at the trace-element level.
